title: Signals updated at wrong times when using verilator.
body:
Using a third-party processor that is from a company that is also an upper body appendage, so cannot provide you a code drop.

Version: Verilator 5.006 2023-01-22 rev v5.006 on Ubuntu-22.04

When running a simulation of the code - a portion of the code is not operating as expected.

First note there are several warnings that are left ignored since this is third-party code.
For this specific code instance where the issue occurs the following warnings are turned off:
`TIMESCALEMOD`, `UNOPTFLAT`, and `UNUSEDSIGNAL` via an included set of `waiver.vlt` files in the file-list which contains:

waiver.vlt eg:
```txt
`verilator_config
lint_off -rule TIMESCALEMOD -file "*<dir>/<filename>.v"
....
etc.
```

The verilator command is as follows:

```bash
/usr/local/bin/verilator_bin --compiler clang --prefix Vtop_core --Mdir Vtop_core.dir --make cmake -Wall -Wpedantic --assert -sv --hierarchical --trace --trace-structs --coverage -DVERILATOR_WORKAROUND=1 --no-timing --top top_core --cc \
-I <root>/...  -I<root>/... etc.  \ # A bunch of these 
<File list - lots of them mix of system verilog and verilog> \
<root>/../waiver.vlt \ # Several of these for various modules - these include lint_off -rule <RULE> -file "*dir/filename.v"
```

It all compiles well and I have not had this issue with any of the designs I've done (although they've been much smaller), that are warning clean - i.e. no warnings to mention.

After compilation I have a C++ testbench environment that uses GoogleTest as the Test Framwork and some UVM like drivers (but written in C++)  that generate the stimulus.  The biggest thing is it doesn't work on every tick but identifies the external minimum tick and updates on the next required change - i.e. time advances multiple ns depending on the inputs driven and when they need to be updated.

That being said this approach has worked for me on several designs so far but is failing me now with this particular verilog code.

there is a flop which is defined as:

```verilog
  always @ (posedge hclk or negedge hreset_n)
    if (!hreset_n)
      foo <= {32{1'b0}};
    else if (foo_we)
      foo <= nxt_foo;
```

note nxt_foo is generated via a large logic and/or tree eg:
```verilog
always @(... list of inputs)
  case(bar_state_i)
   `BAR_STATE_1 : begin
       nxt_foo = 32'hFFFFFFFF;
       foo_we  = ready;
   end
   `BAR_STATE_2, `BAR_STATE_3, BAR_STATE_4 : begin
      nxt_foo = {some_value[31:2], 2'b00};
      foo_we = ready & some_enable;
   end
   .... Several more of these.
   `default : begin
     nxt_foo = {32{1'bx}};
     foo_we = 1'bx;
  endcase
```

And under certain circumstances the waveforms show that on some `posedge hclk` scenarios (not all), the value of `nxt_foo` is updated before the value of foo is captured, meaning for some clock cycles `foo` and `nxt_foo` change to the same value (i.e. what `nxt_foo` updated to).

Sorry for the rudimentary diagram but thought this was simpler than wavedrom decipher if you don't have a viewer.
So at cycle 3 nxt_foo = 0xAAAAAAAA, but that doesn't get updated - cycle 4's value gets updated on both nxt_foo and foo.

I've added what is expected below but that isn't what the verilated C code is generating.

```txt
Cycle            |    0    |    1    |    2    |    3    |    4    |    5    |    6    |
hclk         ____|----|____|----|____|----|____|----|____|----|____|----|____|----|____|----|____
foo_we    _________________|---------|_________|-------------------|____________________
nxt_foo   _________________Xffffffff X_________Xaaaaaaaa X00000123 X____________________
Actual:
foo       ___________________________Xffffffff           X00000123
Expected:
foo       ___________________________Xffffffff           Xaaaaaaaa X00000123
```

How would I go and investigate this bug? - I looked at the verilated code and might disable code coverage to remove all of the unruly stuff that adds - and look at what gets updated first and why - any other options?

Also note with another testbench I have run through this same scenario with Questa Sim (New name for Mentor Graphics) - and it does show the Expected values and differs from what Verilator produces.
